title: Uncertainty — the exam recipe
summary:
A battle-card for the uncertainty part of the exam: the whole GUM workflow compressed into the moves you actually make. **Type A**: $u=s/\sqrt n$, $
u=n-1$. **Type B**: half-width $a$ over the right divisor — rectangular $a/\sqrt3$, triangular $a/\sqrt6$, arcsine $a/\sqrt2$, resolution $a=r/2\Rightarrow u=r/(2\sqrt3)$, expanded $u=U/k$. **Combine** by the law of propagation of variance $u^2(y)=\sum_j c_j^2 u^2(x_j)$ (relative-RSS shortcut for products/quotients/powers). **Expand**: Welch–Satterthwaite $
u_y$ (floored), $k=$T.INV$(1-\alpha/2,
u_y)$, $U=k\,u(y)$, report $y\pm U$ at $P$. Then drill it on 28 exam-style questions and 2 full budgets.
objectives: Decode the wording of any input in one move: a standard deviation is already $u$; a variance needs $\sqrt V$; an expanded uncertainty needs $U/k$; a half-width needs a distribution divisor.
Compute a Type A standard uncertainty $u=s/\sqrt n$ with $\nu=n-1$, and only divide by $\sqrt n$ when the reported result is the average.
Apply the Type B divisor card without hesitation: rectangular $a/\sqrt3$, triangular $a/\sqrt6$, arcsine $a/\sqrt2$, resolution $r/(2\sqrt3)$, quoted expanded value $U/k$.

Combine uncorrelated contributions with $u^2(y)=\sum_j c_j^2 u^2(x_j)$, and use the relative-RSS shortcut $\left(u(y)/y\right)^2=\sum_j p_j^2\left(u(x_j)/x_j\right)^2$ for products, quotients and powers.
Get the effective degrees of freedom by Welch–Satterthwaite (floored) and the coverage factor $k=$T.INV$(1-\alpha/2,\nu_y)$.
Report a complete result $y\pm U$ at a stated confidence $P$, and know when $k=2$ is legitimate and when the Student-$t$ is mandatory.

blocks.text: This is the condensed *exam recipe* for the uncertainty budget — the 12-point heart of the exam. The two theory lessons and Lab 3 teach the machinery; this card is what you replay under time pressure. Every budget, whatever the model, is the same five moves: **classify** each input (Type A or Type B), **standardise** it into a standard uncertainty $u$, **combine** through the sensitivity coefficients, **expand** with the coverage factor, **report**. The drills below hit every place where marks are usually lost.
0. Read the wording: what kind of number is it?

blocks.title: The four-way input decoder

blocks.text: 1. Type A — statistics on your own readings

blocks.caption: Type A standard uncertainty when the reported result is the **average** of $n$ repeated readings; $s$ is the sample standard deviation (STDEV.S, divisor $n-1$). If a *single* reading is reported, $u=s$ with no $\sqrt n$.

blocks.title: √n, not n — and only for an average
blocks.text: Two classic wrong turns: dividing by $n$ instead of $\sqrt n$ (shrinks $u$ far too much), and dividing by $\sqrt n$ when the result is a **single reading**, not a mean. Read whether the quoted variability refers to a mean or to an individual value: they differ by a factor $\sqrt n$ ($n$ on the variance), and mixing them up works in both directions — see the multiplicities $n_d/n_r$ in Lab 3.

blocks.title: Turn any Type B datum into a standard uncertainty

steps.label: Rectangular (uniform)
steps.text: The **default** when nothing is known inside the band $\pm a$ — accuracy classes, declarations of conformity, handbook/tabled values: $u=a/\sqrt3$ (variance $a^2/3$).

steps.label: Resolution
steps.text: A reading step $r$ spans one step, so the **half-width is $a=r/2$**, rectangular: $u=\dfrac{r/2}{\sqrt3}=\dfrac{r}{2\sqrt3}$. Do not use the full step as the half-width.

steps.label: Arcsine (U-shaped)
steps.text: Sinusoidal factors that linger at their extremes — e.g. the day–night swing of an **unconditioned** temperature: $u=a/\sqrt2$ (variance $a^2/2$).

steps.label: Triangular
steps.text: When the centre of the band is credibly more likely than the edges (not recommended for inexpert users): $u=a/\sqrt6$ (variance $a^2/6$).

steps.label: Quoted expanded uncertainty
steps.text: A certificate value $U$ at confidence $P$: $u=U/k$, with $k=t_{1-\alpha/2,\nu}=$ T.INV$(1-(1-P)/2,\nu)$ if the d.o.f. are stated, or $k=2$ for a plain "95%, $2\sigma$" quote.

steps.label: Degrees of freedom
steps.text:
Type A: $
u=n-1$ (regression slope: $
u=n-2$). Type B by convention: $
u=100$ extremely consolidated, $30$ consolidated, $15$ to be checked.

question.prompt: A gauge block certificate states only: "maximum deviation $\pm0.06$ mm". No distribution information is given. Its standard uncertainty is closest to:
question.explanation: Nothing is known inside the band → **rectangular** by default, $u=a/\sqrt3=0.06/1.732=0.0346\approx0.035$ mm. **A** uses no divisor, treating the half-width as if it were a standard deviation. **B** just halves the half-width — not any GUM divisor. **D** applies the triangular divisor $\sqrt6$ ($0.06/2.449=0.024$), which requires a reason to believe the centre is more likely.
question.theory: A bare "$\pm a$" with no further information → rectangular, $u=a/\sqrt3$.

blocks.text: 3. Combine — RSS through the sensitivity coefficients

blocks.tex: u^2(y)=\sum_{j} c_j^2\,u^2(x_j),\qquad c_j=\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial x_j}\right)_{\bar x_j}
blocks.caption: Law of propagation of variance for uncorrelated inputs. Each budget row is $u_j^2(y)=c_j^2u^2(x_j)$; the rows are **summed as variances** and the square root is taken **once**, at the end. Only $c_j^2$ enters — the sign of a sensitivity never matters in the sum.

blocks.title: Variances add, root once
blocks.text: Never add standard uncertainties linearly ($u_1+u_2$ is *wrong*), never subtract them, never root each row. Build the budget row by row in **variance**: $u_j^2(y)=c_j^2u^2(x_j)$, sum, then $u(y)=\sqrt{u^2(y)}$. For $y=kx$: $c_k=x$, $c_x=k$. For a product $y=\prod x_i$: $c_j=y/x_j$. For $V=\pi r^2 h$: $c_r=2\pi r h$, $c_h=\pi r^2$ — the exponent comes down as a factor.

blocks.caption: Relative-uncertainty shortcut for **products, quotients and powers** (a quotient is just $p_j=-1$). It is exactly the propagation law with $c_j=p_j\,y/x_j$ — the constant $K$ drops out. Fastest route for models like $\rho=m/V$ or $V=\pi d^3/6$.

blocks.title: The exponent multiplies the relative uncertainty — and gets squared in the sum
blocks.text: For $y\propto d^3$, the relative contribution of $d$ is $3\,u(d)/d$, i.e. $p^2=9$ times the relative *variance*. The two classic wrong turns: **forgetting the exponent** entirely (using $u(d)/d$), and **squaring the exponent twice** (using $p^2\,u(d)/d=9\,u(d)/d$ as if it were the relative uncertainty). Also remember the shortcut only applies to pure products/quotients/powers — a **sum or difference** must go through the absolute form $u^2(y)=\sum c_j^2u^2(x_j)$.

The combined $u(y)$ is a standard deviation — a $\pm u$ band leaves out more than 30% of the values, so the GUM scales it to a confidence interval. First get one **effective** number of degrees of freedom for $y$ with **Welch–Satterthwaite**, $\nu_y=\left\lfloor u^4(y)\big/\sum_j\left(u_j^4(y)/\nu_j\right)\right\rfloor$ — always the **floor** (Excel INT), never round up: fewer d.o.f. means a fatter-tailed $t$, a larger $k$ and a safely conservative $U$.

blocks.caption: Coverage factor and expanded uncertainty. At 95% with many d.o.f., $k\to1.96\approx2$ (the metrological $2\sigma$ convention); at 90% $\to1.645$, 98% $\to2.33$, 99% $\to2.58$. With a small $\nu_y$ the Student-$t$ value is mandatory ($t_{0.975,4}=2.78$, $t_{0.975,15}=2.13$).

blocks.title: The Student-t behind the coverage factor k
blocks.caption:
Drop the degrees of freedom and watch the 95% two-sided quantile climb above the normal $1.96$: $2.13$ at $
u=15$, $2.78$ at $
u=4$, $3.18$ at $
u=3$. This is why a small-sample Type A budget must never use $k=2$.

blocks.title: The 7-step exam recipe (write it on your wrist)

steps.text: Convert every datum to **base units** (µm→m, g→kg, %→pure number). A mixed-unit budget is numerically wrong even with perfect formulas.
Classify each input: **Type A** (statistics on repeated observations) or **Type B** (certificate, resolution, handbook, experience).
Standardise: $s/\sqrt n$ (A on a mean), $\sqrt V$, $U/k$, or half-width over $\sqrt3/\sqrt2/\sqrt6$ — and note each $\nu_j$.
Sensitivities $c_j=\partial G/\partial x_j$ (analytically or as an incremental ratio); per-row contribution $u_j^2(y)=c_j^2u^2(x_j)$.
$U=k\,u(y)$; report $y\pm U$ at the stated confidence $P$. Name the dominant contribution — that is where money improves the measurement.

blocks.title: The recipe in 60 seconds — a length with two contributions
blocks.text:
A length is the **average of $n=8$ readings** with sample standard deviation $s=0.020$ mm, taken on an instrument with **resolution $r=0.010$ mm**.
- Type A: $u_A=s/\sqrt8=0.00707$ mm, $\nu=7$.
- Type B (resolution, rectangular, $a=r/2$): $u_B=0.010/(2\sqrt3)=0.00289$ mm, $\nu=100$.
- Combine ($c_j=1$): $u^2(y)=5.00\times10^{-5}+8.33\times10^{-6}=5.83\times10^{-5}\ \text{mm}^2$, so $u(y)=0.00764$ mm.
- Welch–Satterthwaite: raw $9.51\to\nu_y=9$; $k=$T.INV$(0.975,9)=2.262$.
- $U=2.262\times0.00764=0.0173$ mm → report $y\pm0.017$ mm at 95%. Note $k=2$ would have understated $U$ by ~12%.

question.prompt: A budget ends with $u(y)=0.50$ mm and effective degrees of freedom $\nu_y=4$. The expanded uncertainty at 95% confidence is closest to:
question.explanation: With $\nu_y=4$ the coverage factor is the Student-$t$ quantile $k=t_{0.975,4}=$T.INV$(0.975,4)=2.776$, so $U=2.776\times0.50=1.39$ mm. **A** uses the asymptotic normal $1.96$ — valid only for large $\nu_y$. **C** uses the metrological $k=2$ convention, which also requires many d.o.f. **D** forgets the coverage factor entirely and reports the standard uncertainty.

blocks.title: The five classic mark-losers
blocks.text:
1. **Forgetting $\sqrt3$** (or using the full resolution step instead of $a=r/2$).
2. **$s/n$ instead of $s/\sqrt n$** — or applying $\sqrt n$ when a single reading is reported.
3. **Adding uncertainties linearly** instead of summing variances (RSS).
4. **Dropping the exponent** (or its square) in the relative shortcut for powers.
5. **$k=2$ with a small $\nu_y$**, or rounding $\nu_y$ **up** instead of flooring — both understate $U$.
And always: base units before anything else.

practice.prompt: A current sensor's datasheet states only: "maximum error $\pm0.2$ mA". No distribution or confidence information is given. Which model do you adopt for this Type B input?
practice.explanation: When **nothing is known** about the behaviour of the error inside its band, every value is equally likely → the **rectangular (uniform)** distribution is the default, $u=a/\sqrt3$. **A** invents a normal model with a made-up coverage factor — a datasheet limit is a half-width, not an expanded uncertainty. **B** (triangular) requires a reason to believe the centre is more likely, and is not recommended for inexpert users. **D** (arcsine) is reserved for sinusoidal factors that linger at their extremes, like an unconditioned temperature.
practice.theory: Default for a bare "$\pm a$" spec with no further information: rectangular, $u=a/\sqrt3$.

practice.prompt: A voltmeter datasheet gives a maximum error of $\pm0.15$ V, to be treated as rectangular. The standard uncertainty is closest to:
practice.explanation: Rectangular with half-width $a=0.15$ V: $u=a/\sqrt3=0.15/1.732=0.0866\approx0.087$ V. **A** forgets the divisor and reports the half-width itself. **C** uses the triangular divisor $\sqrt6$ ($0.15/2.449=0.061$). **D** treats the spec like a resolution, halving first and then dividing by $\sqrt3$ ($0.15/(2\sqrt3)=0.043$) — but a "$\pm a$" spec is *already* a half-width; only a resolution step $r$ needs the extra halving.
practice.theory: Rectangular Type B: $u=a/\sqrt3$, where $a$ is the half-width of the stated band.

practice.prompt: For a two-sided 95% confidence level and **many** degrees of freedom, the coverage factor is:
practice.explanation: As $\nu_y\to\infty$ the Student-$t$ quantile tends to the normal $z_{0.975}=1.96$, rounded to $k=2$ by the metrological $2\sigma$ convention. **A** is the 90% two-sided factor ($z_{0.95}=1.645$). **B** is the 99% factor ($z_{0.995}=2.58$). **C** is the $3\sigma$ level ($\approx99.7\%$), not the 95% convention.
practice.theory: Large-$\nu$ two-sided coverage factors: 90% → 1.645, 95% → 1.96 ≈ 2, 98% → 2.33, 99% → 2.58.

practice.prompt: Sixteen repeated readings have sample standard deviation $s=2.4$ mm, and the reported result is their **average**. The standard uncertainty of the result is:
practice.explanation: Type A on a mean: $u=s/\sqrt n=2.4/\sqrt{16}=2.4/4=0.60$ mm (with $\nu=15$). **B** is $s$ itself — right only if a *single* reading were reported. **C** divides by $n=16$ instead of $\sqrt n$, the classic wrong turn. **D** multiplies by $\sqrt n$ instead of dividing — averaging *reduces* the uncertainty of the mean, never inflates it.
practice.theory: Type A for an average of $n$ readings: $u=s/\sqrt n$, $\nu=n-1$.

practice.prompt: Two independent (uncorrelated) contributions to the same result, already in output units, are $u_1(y)=0.6$ mm and $u_2(y)=0.8$ mm. The combined standard uncertainty is:
practice.explanation: Variances add: $u^2(y)=0.6^2+0.8^2=0.36+0.64=1.00\ \text{mm}^2$, so $u(y)=1.00$ mm. **A** adds the standard uncertainties linearly ($0.6+0.8$), which overstates the combination — RSS is always $\le$ the linear sum. **B** averages them, which has no statistical meaning here. **D** subtracts them — uncertainties never subtract.
practice.theory: Uncorrelated contributions combine in quadrature (RSS): $u(y)=\sqrt{u_1^2+u_2^2}$.

practice.prompt: You weigh a part 10 times and average the readings; the balance's calibration certificate also quotes a bias band. How do these two pieces of information enter the budget?

options.content: Both are Type A, since both concern the same balance
The repeated readings are Type A; the certificate band is Type B — and both end up as standard uncertainties $u$
The readings are Type B (they contain random errors); the certificate is Type A
Only the larger of the two is kept; the other is discarded

practice.explanation: The classification follows the **method of evaluation**: statistics on repeated observations → Type A ($u=s/\sqrt{10}$, $\nu=9$); prior knowledge from a certificate → Type B (band over the right divisor). Both are then just standard uncertainties and combine identically. **A** and **C** tie the types to the instrument or to random/systematic character — the classic misconception; the GUM types refer only to *how* the contribution is evaluated. **D** is wrong: independent contributions are combined by RSS, never cherry-picked.
practice.theory: Type A = statistical analysis of repeated observations; Type B = any other means. Both yield $u$ and combine by the same propagation law.

practice.prompt: A balance certificate quotes an expanded uncertainty $U=0.24$ g with coverage factor $k=2$. The standard uncertainty to put in the budget is:
practice.explanation: An expanded uncertainty is a coverage-scaled value: $u=U/k=0.24/2=0.12$ g. **A** forgets to remove the coverage and enters the expanded value directly, doubling this row's weight in the budget. **B** multiplies by $k$ instead of dividing — that direction is for *producing* $U$ at the end, not for reading a certificate. **C** applies the rectangular divisor $\sqrt3$ ($0.24/1.732$), but a certificate's $U$ is not a half-width of a uniform band.
practice.theory: Quoted expanded uncertainty → standard: $u=U/k$.

practice.prompt: A digital multimeter displays voltage with a resolution of $0.01$ V. The standard uncertainty contributed by the resolution is closest to:
practice.explanation: The true value lies anywhere within one resolution step, so the half-width is $a=r/2=0.005$ V, rectangular: $u=0.005/\sqrt3=0.01/(2\sqrt3)=0.00289$ V. **B** uses the full step as the half-width ($0.01/\sqrt3=0.0058$), double-counting the spread. **C** stops at the half-width $r/2$ without the $\sqrt3$ divisor. **D** treats the whole step as if it were the standard uncertainty.
practice.theory: Resolution $r$: rectangular with $a=r/2$, so $u=r/(2\sqrt3)$.

practice.prompt: An assembly clearance is believed to lie in $\pm0.9$ mm with the **central value more likely** than the extremes, so a triangular distribution is adopted. The standard uncertainty is closest to:
practice.explanation: Triangular over half-width $a=0.9$ mm: $u=a/\sqrt6=0.9/2.449=0.367$ mm. **A** uses the rectangular divisor $\sqrt3$ ($0.9/1.732=0.52$) — that would ignore the extra knowledge that the centre is more likely. **C** uses the arcsine divisor $\sqrt2$ ($0.64$), which models the *opposite* (probability piled at the edges). **D** simply halves $a$, which is no GUM divisor. Remember the ranking for equal $a$: arcsine > rectangular > triangular.
practice.theory: Triangular Type B: $u=a/\sqrt6$ (variance $a^2/6$) — the smallest of the three divisor-based variances.

practice.prompt: Five repeated readings have sample standard deviation $s=0.80\ \mu$m and the reported result is their average. The Type A standard uncertainty is closest to:
practice.explanation: $u=s/\sqrt n=0.80/\sqrt5=0.358\approx0.36\ \mu$m, with $\nu=n-1=4$ degrees of freedom (keep that $\nu$ — it will make the coverage factor a Student-$t$ value later). **A** divides by $n=5$ instead of $\sqrt5$. **B** forgets the averaging entirely. **D** multiplies by $\sqrt5$ — the wrong direction.
practice.theory: Type A on a mean: $u=s/\sqrt n$, $\nu=n-1$; small $n$ ⇒ few d.o.f. ⇒ Student-$t$ coverage later.

practice.prompt: In which situation is the coverage factor **required** to come from the Student-$t$ distribution rather than the normal value $1.96$ (or the conventional $k=2$)?

options.content: When the effective degrees of freedom $\nu_y$ are small — e.g. the budget is dominated by a Type A contribution from few readings
Whenever any input is evaluated as Type B
Whenever the confidence level is raised from 95% to 99%
When the measurand itself is not normally distributed

practice.explanation:
The Student-$t$ is needed because the combined $u(y)$ is itself *estimated*; with **few effective degrees of freedom** its estimate is unreliable and the $t$ tails are fatter, so $k=t_{1-\alpha/2,
u_y}>1.96$ (e.g. $2.78$ at $
u_y=4$). Using $1.96$ there would understate $U$. **B** is backwards — Type B contributions usually carry *large* conventional d.o.f. (100/30/15), pushing $
u_y$ up, towards $k	o z$. **C** changes the quantile level, not the distribution — 99% with many d.o.f. is simply $2.58$. **D** confuses the output's distribution (taken normal via the CLT) with the estimation problem that forces the $t$.
practice.theory:
$k=t_{1-\alpha/2,
u_y}$; it exceeds the normal $z$ for finite $
u_y$ and tends to it as $
u_y	o\infty$.

practice.prompt: A budget has a Type A contribution $u_A=0.35$ mm and an independent Type B contribution $u_B=0.29$ mm (both already in output units, $c_j=1$). The combined standard uncertainty is closest to:
practice.explanation: Type A and Type B are both standard uncertainties, so they combine identically: $u^2(y)=0.35^2+0.29^2=0.1225+0.0841=0.2066$, $u(y)=0.455\approx0.45$ mm. **A** adds them linearly ($0.35+0.29=0.64$). **B** averages them. **C** subtracts the variances ($\sqrt{0.35^2-0.29^2}=0.196$) — uncertainties never subtract. Note the combined value always lies between the largest contribution and the linear sum.
practice.theory: Once everything is a standard uncertainty, Type A and B combine by the same RSS: $u(y)=\sqrt{u_A^2+u_B^2}$.

practice.prompt: A cylinder volume is modelled as $V=\pi r^2 h$ with $r=10$ mm and $h=50$ mm. The sensitivity coefficient of the **radius**, $c_r=\partial V/\partial r$, is closest to:
practice.explanation: $c_r=\partial V/\partial r=2\pi r h=2\pi\cdot10\cdot50=3142\ \text{mm}^2$ — the squared variable brings its exponent down as a factor 2. **A** is $\pi r^2=314\ \text{mm}^2$, which is $c_h$ (the sensitivity of the *height*). **C** is $\pi rh$ — the factor 2 from the exponent was dropped. **D** is $2\pi r$, forgetting $h$ altogether. Because $r$ appears squared, its uncertainty weighs $2^2=4$ times more (in relative variance) than $h$'s.
practice.theory: Differentiate the model: for $V=\pi r^2h$, $c_r=2\pi rh$ and $c_h=\pi r^2$; an exponent $p$ appears as the factor $p$ in the sensitivity.

practice.prompt: Electrical power is computed as $P=VI$. The inputs have relative standard uncertainties $u(V)/V=0.5\%$ and $u(I)/I=1.2\%$, uncorrelated. The relative standard uncertainty of $P$ is:
practice.explanation: For a product, relative variances add: $(u(P)/P)^2=0.5^2+1.2^2=0.25+1.44=1.69$, so $u(P)/P=\sqrt{1.69}=1.3\%$ exactly. **B** adds the relative uncertainties linearly ($0.5+1.2$). **C** multiplies them ($0.5\times1.2$), which is meaningless. **D** subtracts the variances ($\sqrt{1.44-0.25}=1.09$) — combination is always a sum for uncorrelated inputs.
practice.theory: Products/quotients: $(u(y)/y)^2=\sum(u(x_j)/x_j)^2$ (exponents $\pm1$).

practice.prompt: A sphere volume is $V=\pi d^3/6$ and the diameter is known with relative standard uncertainty $u(d)/d=0.4\%$. The relative standard uncertainty of $V$ is:
practice.explanation: For a power $y\propto d^p$, the relative uncertainty scales by $|p|$: $u(V)/V=3\times0.4\%=1.2\%$ (the constant $\pi/6$ carries no uncertainty). **A** forgets the exponent entirely. **B** multiplies by $p^2=9$ — the square belongs inside the *variance* sum, $(u(V)/V)^2=3^2(u(d)/d)^2$, not on the relative uncertainty itself. **C** divides by the exponent instead of multiplying.
practice.theory: Single power $y=Kx^p$: $u(y)/y=|p|\,u(x)/x$; in the variance sum the exponent enters as $p^2$.

practice.prompt: A reproducibility contribution is quoted as a **variance** of $0.0625\ \text{mm}^2$ (referring to a single measurement, and a single measurement is reported). What standard uncertainty enters the budget?
practice.explanation: A variance needs one square root: $u=\sqrt{V}=\sqrt{0.0625}=0.25$ mm. **A** copies the variance as if it were a standard uncertainty — wrong value *and* wrong units ($\text{mm}^2$ vs mm). **B** halves the variance, which is no operation of the decoder. **D** applies the rectangular divisor $\sqrt3$ — divisors are for half-widths, not variances. No $\sqrt n$ correction applies because both the datum and the result refer to a single measurement.
practice.theory: Input decoder: variance → $u=\sqrt V$; std deviation → $u=s$; expanded → $u=U/k$; half-width → divisor.

practice.prompt: An exercise gives you the **reproducibility** of the measurement (from a test that repeated the whole procedure, operators included) *and* the **repeatability** of the same instrument. How do they enter the budget?

options.content: Both as separate rows — more rows means a more complete budget
Only the reproducibility: it already includes the repeatability, so adding both double-counts the random scatter
Only the repeatability: it is the more fundamental property
Their average, as a single row

practice.explanation: Reproducibility is the scatter of the *complete* procedure — it contains the instrument's repeatability inside it. Entering both inflates $u(y)$ by counting the same physical scatter twice; the professor's sheets mark repeatability "*not present*" when reproducibility is given. **A** confuses completeness with correctness — a budget row must be an *independent* contribution. **C** throws away the larger, more representative information. **D** has no statistical basis; contributions are never averaged.
practice.theory: Never double-count: if reproducibility (which embeds repeatability) is in the budget, repeatability is marked not present.

practice.prompt: What is the **purpose** of the Welch–Satterthwaite formula in the GUM workflow?

options.content: To combine the input variances into $u^2(y)$
To convert Type B contributions into Type A ones
To compute the sensitivity coefficients numerically
To assign one effective number of degrees of freedom $\nu_y$ to the combined $u(y)$, so a single coverage factor can be read from the Student-$t$

practice.explanation:
The budget mixes contributions of different reliability ($
u=n-1$, $n-2$ for a regression slope, conventional 100/30/15 for Type B). Welch–Satterthwaite condenses them into one **effective** $
u_y=\lfloor u^4(y)/\sum_j(u_j^4(y)/
u_j)floor$, which then fixes $k=$T.INV$(1-\alpha/2,
u_y)$. **A** is the law of propagation of variance — a different, earlier step. **B** is impossible: the types describe how a value was *evaluated*, and nothing converts one into the other. **C** is the incremental-ratio technique, unrelated.
practice.theory: W–S: $\nu_y=\lfloor u^4(y)/\sum_j(u_j^4(y)/\nu_j)\rfloor$ — the bridge between the budget and the coverage factor.

practice.prompt: Measurements are taken in an **air-conditioned** laboratory whose control keeps the temperature within $\pm1\,^{\circ}$C of the set point, with no further information on its behaviour inside the band. The temperature input is modelled as:
practice.explanation: Inside a *conditioned* band with nothing else known, every value is equally likely → **rectangular**, $u=a/\sqrt3=0.58\,^{\circ}$C. **B** is the model for an **unconditioned** temperature swinging sinusoidally day–night, which lingers at the extremes — the opposite situation. **C** would need a reason to prefer the centre; the controller specification gives none. **D** invents a normal model and a coverage factor that the specification never stated.
practice.theory: Conditioned temperature band → rectangular ($a/\sqrt3$); unconditioned sinusoidal swing → arcsine ($a/\sqrt2$).

practice.prompt: Which of the following inputs is evaluated as **Type B**?

options.content: The sample standard deviation of 20 repeated readings
The variance obtained from a repeatability test on the same part
The bias band of an instrument declared conforming to an accuracy class, with no calibration data
The standard uncertainty of a regression slope fitted to calibration data

practice.explanation: A declaration of conformity gives only a *variability interval* known from prior information — no statistics of your own — so it is evaluated by "other means": **Type B**, rectangular by default. **A** and **B** are textbook Type A: statistical analysis of repeated observations. **D** is also statistical — a regression is fitted to measured data, and its slope uncertainty carries $\nu=n-2$ degrees of freedom, a Type A signature.
practice.theory: Type B = evaluated from prior knowledge (certificates, classes, handbooks, resolution); Type A = statistics on observations (including regression, $\nu=n-n_p$).

practice.prompt: A data sheet states, with no qualifier: "uncertainty: $0.5$ mm". According to the course convention, how do you read this value, and what does a *complete* uncertainty statement require?

options.content: It is a standard uncertainty; a complete statement needs only $y$ and $u$
It is an **expanded** uncertainty; a complete statement reports $y\pm U$ with the confidence level $P$ (and the d.o.f. used)
It is a variance; a complete statement reports $y\pm s^2$
It is a rectangular half-width; a complete statement reports $y\pm a/\sqrt3$

practice.explanation: The convention is explicit: *if a value is quoted simply as "uncertainty", it is to be read as the expanded uncertainty* $U$. And the expression of a result is complete only as $y\pm U$ **with the confidence level $P$** (and the degrees of freedom) stated. **A** would make budgets built on that number too optimistic by the coverage factor. **C** is dimensionally wrong ($\text{mm}^2$). **D** confuses a finished, coverage-scaled uncertainty with a raw Type B band.
practice.theory: Bare "uncertainty" = expanded $U$; complete report = $y\pm U$ at stated $P$ (with d.o.f.).

practice.prompt: A voltmeter's accuracy specification is "$\pm0.2\%$ **of full scale**" on the 10 V range (rectangular). You read $4.00$ V. The standard uncertainty of the reading is closest to:
practice.explanation: "Of full scale" means the half-width is computed on the range, not the reading: $a=0.002\times10\ \text{V}=0.02$ V, so $u=a/\sqrt3=11.5$ mV — the same absolute value anywhere on the range. **B** applies the percentage to the *reading* ($0.002\times4/\sqrt3=4.6$ mV), the classic misread; that would be correct only for a "% of reading" spec. **C** treats the band like a resolution and halves it again ($0.02/(2\sqrt3)$) — the spec is already a half-width. **D** forgets the $\sqrt3$ divisor.
practice.theory: Read the reference of a relative spec: "% of full scale" → $a=\%\times$range; "% of reading" → $a=\%\times$reading. Then rectangular $u=a/\sqrt3$.

practice.prompt: A budget has two contributions: $u_1^2(y)=0.30\ \mu\text{m}^2$ with $\nu_1=4$, and $u_2^2(y)=0.20\ \mu\text{m}^2$ with $\nu_2=30$. The effective degrees of freedom by Welch–Satterthwaite are:
practice.explanation:
$
u_y=\dfrac{u^4(y)}{u_1^4/
u_1+u_2^4/
u_2}=\dfrac{(0.30+0.20)^2}{0.30^2/4+0.20^2/30}=\dfrac{0.25}{0.0225+0.00133}=10.49$, floored to $
u_y=10$. **A** simply adds the d.o.f. ($4+30$) — W–S never does that. **B** rounds $10.49$ up; the convention is the **floor** (Excel INT), because rounding up shrinks $k$ and understates $U$. **D** takes the smallest input d.o.f., which is over-conservative and not the GUM rule.
practice.theory: W–S: $\nu_y=\lfloor u^4(y)/\sum_j(u_j^4(y)/\nu_j)\rfloor$ — note $u^4$ means the *variance squared*, and the result is floored.

practice.prompt: A force is modelled as $F=kx$ with $k=500$ N/m, $u(k)=4$ N/m, $x=0.020$ m, $u(x)=0.0002$ m (uncorrelated). The combined standard uncertainty $u(F)$ is closest to:
practice.explanation: Sensitivities: $c_k=\partial F/\partial k=x=0.020$ m, $c_x=\partial F/\partial x=k=500$ N/m. Contributions: $(0.020\times4)^2=0.08^2=0.0064$ and $(500\times0.0002)^2=0.10^2=0.0100$, so $u(F)=\sqrt{0.0164}=0.128\approx0.13$ N. **A** adds the two contributions linearly ($0.08+0.10$). **B** combines the raw input uncertainties without sensitivity coefficients ($\sqrt{4^2+0.0002^2}\approx4$) — a unit-inconsistent nonsense. **C** subtracts the variances ($\sqrt{0.0100-0.0064}$). Note both contributions are comparable, so neither input can be neglected.
practice.theory: For $y=kx$: $c_k=x$, $c_x=k$; each row is $(c_ju(x_j))^2$ and rows add as variances.

practice.prompt: A density is modelled as $\rho=\dfrac{4m}{\pi d^2 h}$, with relative standard uncertainties $u(m)/m=0.1\%$, $u(d)/d=0.3\%$, $u(h)/h=0.2\%$ (uncorrelated). The relative standard uncertainty of $\rho$ is closest to:
practice.explanation: Exponents: $m^{+1}$, $d^{-2}$, $h^{-1}$. Relative variances add with $p_j^2$: $(u(\rho)/\rho)^2=0.1^2+(2\times0.3)^2+0.2^2=0.01+0.36+0.04=0.41$, so $u(\rho)/\rho=\sqrt{0.41}=0.64\%$. **A** forgets the exponent 2 on the diameter ($\sqrt{0.01+0.09+0.04}=0.37\%$) — the most tempting wrong turn. **C** adds linearly with the factor 2 ($0.1+0.6+0.2$). **D** keeps only the dominant diameter term ($2\times0.3$) and drops the others. The sign of the exponents ($-2$, $-1$) is irrelevant: only $p_j^2$ enters.
practice.theory: $(u(y)/y)^2=\sum p_j^2(u(x_j)/x_j)^2$; a squared variable in the denominator contributes with $p^2=4$.

practice.prompt: A resistance is measured $n=5$ times: sample standard deviation $s=1.2\ \Omega$, the mean is reported, and Type A is the only contribution. The expanded uncertainty at 95% confidence is closest to:
practice.explanation: $u=s/\sqrt5=0.537\ \Omega$ with $\nu=4$; the coverage factor is the Student-$t$ quantile $k=t_{0.975,4}=$T.INV$(0.975,4)=2.776$, so $U=2.776\times0.537=1.49\ \Omega$. **B** uses the asymptotic $1.96$ ($1.05\ \Omega$) — with only 4 d.o.f. that understates $U$ by ~30%. **C** forgets the $\sqrt5$ and expands $s$ itself ($2.776\times1.2$). **D** uses the one-sided quantile $t_{0.95,4}=2.132$ instead of the two-sided $t_{0.975,4}$ — a 95% *two-sided* interval splits the risk into $2.5\%$ per tail.
practice.theory: Small-sample Type A: $u=s/\sqrt n$, $\nu=n-1$, $k=t_{1-\alpha/2,\nu}$ (two-sided), $U=k\,u$.

practice.prompt: A certificate quotes a standard deviation $s(m)=0.05$ mm that refers to the **mean of $n_d=4$ readings**. Your procedure reports a **single** reading. What standard uncertainty does this contribution give your result?

options.content: $0.05$ mm — use the quoted value directly

practice.explanation: The quoted variability refers to a *mean*: $s^2(m)=s^2(x)/n_d$, so the single-value standard deviation is $s(x)=s(m)\sqrt{n_d}=0.05\times2=0.10$ mm — that is what a single reading inherits. **A** uses the mean's (smaller) scatter for an individual value, understating the row by half. **B** divides again, compounding the error. **C** multiplies the *standard deviation* by $n_d$; the factor $n_d$ belongs on the **variance** ($s^2(x)=n_d\,s^2(m)$), so the standard deviation takes $\sqrt{n_d}$.
practice.theory: Input multiplicity: $s^2(x)=n_d\,s^2(m)$. Mean-referred and single-value variability differ by $n$ on the variance, $\sqrt n$ on $u$.

practice.prompt: A finished budget shows three contributions: $u_1^2(y)=9.0$, $u_2^2(y)=1.0$, $u_3^2(y)=0.25$ (all $\mu\text{m}^2$), so $u(y)=3.20\ \mu$m. You can spend money to halve the standard uncertainty of exactly **one** input. Which choice, and why?

options.content: Input 3 — improving the smallest term is cheapest and always most efficient
Spread the effort equally — all contributions matter the same in an RSS
Input 1 — contributions add as variances, so the dominant row rules: halving $u_1$ drops $u(y)$ from $3.20$ to $1.87\ \mu$m, while halving $u_3$ leaves it at $3.17\ \mu$m
Impossible to say without knowing the sensitivity coefficients

practice.explanation: Because variances add, the budget is a tyranny of the largest term. Halving $u_1$: $u(y)=\sqrt{9/4+1+0.25}=\sqrt{3.5}=1.87\ \mu$m — a 42% improvement. Halving $u_3$ instead: $\sqrt{9+1+0.0625}=3.17\ \mu$m — barely a 1% change. **A** is exactly backwards: small rows are where you can *relax* the instrument to save cost. **B** ignores the quadratic weighting. **D** is a distractor: the listed $u_j^2(y)$ are the **contributions to $y$**, i.e. $c_j^2u^2(x_j)$ — the sensitivities are already inside them.
practice.theory: Read the budget's $u_j^2(y)$ column: improve the biggest row to reduce $u(y)$; relax the smallest rows to cut cost.

exam.title: TU — density of a steel cylinder (full budget, k = 2)
exam.statement:
The density of a cylinder is determined as $\rho=m/V$.

- **Mass**: $n=10$ repeated weighings give an average $\bar m=158.42$ g with sample standard deviation $s=0.10$ g. The balance's calibration certificate also quotes a bias as an **expanded uncertainty $U=0.04$ g with $k=2$**.
- **Volume**: $V=20.00\ \text{cm}^3$, measured with an instrument whose **resolution is $0.05\ \text{cm}^3$** (no other volume contribution).

Evaluate every standard uncertainty, combine them into the **relative** combined standard uncertainty of $\rho$, compute the expanded uncertainty at 95% confidence using the metrological convention $k=2$, report the result, and identify the dominant contribution.
exam.given: $\bar m=158.42$ g; $s=0.10$ g on $n=10$ (mean reported); certificate $U=0.04$ g, $k=2$; $V=20.00\ \text{cm}^3$; resolution $r=0.05\ \text{cm}^3$; $P=95\%$, $k=2$.

steps.title: 1 · Classify the inputs
2 · Standard uncertainties of the mass
steps.content:
Type A on the mean:
$$u_A(m)=\frac{s}{\sqrt n}=\frac{0.10}{\sqrt{10}}=0.0316\ \text{g},\qquad \nu=n-1=9.$$
Certificate bias (expanded → standard):
$$u_{cert}(m)=\frac{U}{k}=\frac{0.04}{2}=0.02\ \text{g}.$$
Both act on the same input, independently, so they combine in quadrature **on the input**:
$$u(m)=\sqrt{0.0316^2+0.02^2}=\sqrt{0.0014}=0.0374\ \text{g}.$$
**Trap:** entering the certificate's $0.04$ g without dividing by $k$ doubles that row.

steps.title: 3 · Standard uncertainty of the volume
steps.content:
The resolution step is $r=0.05\ \text{cm}^3$, so the half-width is $a=r/2=0.025\ \text{cm}^3$, rectangular:
$$u(V)=\frac{r}{2\sqrt3}=\frac{0.05}{2\sqrt3}=0.0144\ \text{cm}^3.$$
**Trap:** using the full step as the half-width ($0.05/\sqrt3=0.0289$) doubles this contribution.

steps.title: 4 · Combine in relative terms
steps.content:
The model $\rho=m/V$ is a pure quotient (exponents $+1$ and $-1$), so the relative-RSS shortcut applies:
$$\left(\frac{u(\rho)}{\rho}\right)^2=\left(\frac{u(m)}{m}\right)^2+\left(\frac{u(V)}{V}\right)^2.$$
Numerically: $u(m)/m=0.0374/158.42=2.36\times10^{-4}$ and $u(V)/V=0.0144/20.00=7.22\times10^{-4}$, hence
$$\frac{u(\rho)}{\rho}=\sqrt{(2.36\times10^{-4})^2+(7.22\times10^{-4})^2}=7.59\times10^{-4}.$$

steps.title: 5 · Absolute combined uncertainty (and a cross-check)
steps.content:
$$\rho=\frac{158.42}{20.00}=7.921\ \text{g/cm}^3,\qquad u(\rho)=7.921\times7.59\times10^{-4}=6.01\times10^{-3}\ \text{g/cm}^3.$$
Cross-check by sensitivities: $c_m=\partial\rho/\partial m=1/V=0.05\ \text{cm}^{-3}$, $c_V=\partial\rho/\partial V=-m/V^2=-0.396$; then $u^2(\rho)=(0.05\times0.0374)^2+(0.396\times0.0144)^2=3.62\times10^{-5}$, i.e. $u(\rho)=6.01\times10^{-3}$ — identical. The negative $c_V$ enters **squared**, so its sign is irrelevant.

steps.title: 6 · Expand and report
steps.content:
At $P=95\%$ with the metrological convention $k=2$ (the Type B contributions carry large conventional d.o.f., so $\nu_y$ is large and $k\approx2$ is legitimate):
$$U=k\,u(\rho)=2\times6.01\times10^{-3}=0.012\ \text{g/cm}^3.$$
Report: $$\rho=(7.921\pm0.012)\ \text{g/cm}^3\ \text{at 95% confidence } (k=2).$$
**Dominant contribution:** the volume resolution — its relative variance $(7.22\times10^{-4})^2$ is $\approx90\%$ of the total, the mass only $\approx10\%$. To improve the measurement, buy a better volume determination, not a better balance.

exam.finalAnswer: $u(m)=0.0374$ g (Type A $0.0316$ g on the mean + certificate $0.020$ g); $u(V)=0.0144\ \text{cm}^3$; relative combined $u(\rho)/\rho=7.59\times10^{-4}$; $u(\rho)=0.0060\ \text{g/cm}^3$; $U=0.012\ \text{g/cm}^3$. **Report: $\rho=(7.921\pm0.012)\ \text{g/cm}^3$ at 95% ($k=2$).** Dominant contribution: the volume resolution ($\approx90\%$ of the variance).
exam.tips: The three decoder moves in one problem: $s/\sqrt n$ for the Type A mean (÷$\sqrt{10}$, not ÷10), $U/k$ for the certificate (never enter the expanded value raw), $r/(2\sqrt3)$ for the resolution (half-step!). Relative uncertainties combine by RSS — adding them linearly ($9.6\times10^{-4}$) loses the marks of step 4. The quotient's $-1$ exponent and the negative $c_V$ disappear when squared.

exam.title: TU — pin diameter with five readings (Student-t coverage)
exam.statement:
The diameter of a pin is measured $n=5$ times with a micrometer whose **resolution is $0.001$ mm**. The readings (mm) are:
$$12.502,\quad 12.508,\quad 12.505,\quad 12.510,\quad 12.500$$
The reported result is the **average**. Build the budget (Type A + resolution), find the effective degrees of freedom by Welch–Satterthwaite, choose the coverage factor at 95% confidence, and report $d\pm U$.
exam.given: 5 readings as above; resolution $r=0.001$ mm (rectangular, $\nu=100$ conventional); result = mean of the 5 readings; $P=95\%$ two-sided.

steps.title: 1 · Type A: mean and standard deviation of the sample
steps.content:
With AVERAGE and STDEV.S (divisor $n-1$):
$$\bar d=\frac{62.525}{5}=12.5050\ \text{mm},\qquad s=\sqrt{\frac{\sum(d_i-\bar d)^2}{4}}=\sqrt{\frac{6.8\times10^{-5}}{4}}=0.00412\ \text{mm}.$$
The mean is reported, so
$$u_A=\frac{s}{\sqrt5}=0.00184\ \text{mm},\qquad \nu_A=n-1=4.$$

steps.title: 3 · Combine
steps.content:
Both sensitivities are 1 (the model is $d=\bar d$ plus a resolution error), so
$$u^2(y)=u_A^2+u_B^2=3.40\times10^{-6}+8.33\times10^{-8}=3.48\times10^{-6}\ \text{mm}^2,$$
$$u(y)=0.00187\ \text{mm}.$$
The resolution is only $\approx2.4\%$ of the combined variance — but it still gets its budget row.

steps.title: 4 · Effective degrees of freedom (Welch–Satterthwaite)
steps.content:
$$
u_y=\left\lfloor rac{u^4(y)}{\dfrac{u_A^4}{
u_A}+\dfrac{u_B^4}{
u_B}}ightfloor=\left\lfloor rac{(3.48	imes10^{-6})^2}{\dfrac{(3.40	imes10^{-6})^2}{4}+\dfrac{(8.33	imes10^{-8})^2}{100}}ightfloor=\lfloor4.198floor=4.$$
The Type A term dominates, so $
u_y$ stays close to its $
u_A=4$. Remember: **floor** (INT), never round up.

steps.title: 5 · Coverage factor from the Student-t
steps.content:
With only 4 effective degrees of freedom the asymptotic $1.96$ (or $k=2$) is **not** allowed:
$$k=t_{0.975,4}=\text{T.INV}(0.975,\ 4)=2.776.$$

steps.title: 6 · Expand and report
steps.content:
$$U=k\,u(y)=2.776\times0.001866=0.0052\ \text{mm}.$$
Report: $$d=(12.5050\pm0.0052)\ \text{mm at 95% confidence } (\nu_y=4,\ k=2.776).$$
Had you used $k=2$, you would have reported $U=0.0037$ mm — an interval **28% too narrow**: exactly the underestimation the Student-$t$ exists to prevent.

exam.tips: Use STDEV.S (divisor $n-1$), not STDEV.P. Divide by $\sqrt5$ because the *mean* is reported. Resolution takes the half-step: $0.001/(2\sqrt3)$, not $0.001/\sqrt3$. Floor the Welch–Satterthwaite value ($4.198\to4$) and read $k$ from T.INV$(0.975,\nu_y)$ — the pair "$k=1.96$/$k=2$ with tiny $\nu_y$" is the most expensive mistake in this problem.
